questions.question:
The equation 7x^7 + 14x^6 + 3x^4 + 12x^3 + 12x^2 + 5x + 7 = 0 has

A. All complex roots
B. At least one real root
C. Four pairs of imaginary roots
D. None of the above
questions.answer: B. At least one real root
questions.to_find: Nature of roots of the degree-7 polynomial

understand.plain: A polynomial of odd degree with real coefficients must cross the x-axis at least once, because it tends to +infinity on one side and -infinity on the other side. By the Intermediate Value Theorem there must be at least one real crossing point. Additionally, complex roots of real-coefficient polynomials always appear in conjugate pairs, so a degree-7 polynomial can have at most 6 complex roots and must have at least 1 real root.

keywords.explain: A polynomial whose highest power is odd. Its end behaviour has opposite signs on the left and right, so it must change sign and therefore cross zero at least once.
keywords.example: p(x) = x^7 + 1: p(-2) < 0 and p(0) = 1 > 0, so a real root lies between -2 and 0.

keywords.explain: For a polynomial with real coefficients, non-real complex roots come in conjugate pairs a+bi and a-bi. Each pair uses up 2 degree slots, so an odd-degree polynomial cannot have all its roots in such pairs.
keywords.example: Degree 7 with 3 conjugate pairs uses only 6 degrees; the 7th root must be real.

keywords.explain: A continuous function that is positive at one point and negative at another must be zero somewhere between those points.
keywords.example: p(0) = 7 > 0 and p(x) -> -infinity as x -> -infinity, so a real root exists for some x < 0.

understand.visual_alt: A smooth degree-7 curve falling from top-right to bottom-left (or the reverse), crossing the x-axis at least once.

given.aim: Determine whether the degree-7 real-coefficient polynomial has any real roots.

terms.example: p(0) = 7 > 0; as x -> -infinity, the leading term 7x^7 -> -infinity so p(x) < 0 for large negative x
terms.connects: The sign change guarantees a real root by IVT

terms.meaning: The highest power present, which is 7, an odd number
terms.example: The leading term is 7x^7, so the polynomial tends to opposite infinities as x -> +/- infinity
terms.connects: Odd degree is the structural reason at least one real root must exist

terms.example: Since all coefficients are real, any complex root must pair with its conjugate
terms.connects: Pairs consume degree in steps of 2; odd degree cannot be fully paired

given.plan: Observe that the polynomial has odd degree (7) and real coefficients. Complex roots must come in conjugate pairs (consuming degree in steps of 2), so an odd-degree polynomial must have at least one real root. Confirm with sign analysis: p(0) = 7 > 0 and p(x) -> -infinity as x -> -infinity, so the IVT guarantees a real root.

steps.title: Sign analysis at two points
steps.apply: p(0) = 7 > 0. For very large negative x the leading term 7x^7 dominates: 7*(-M)^7 = -7M^7 < 0, so p(x) < 0 for sufficiently large M. There is a sign change.
steps.note: A single sign change is enough to invoke IVT.

steps.title: Conclude via IVT and conjugate-pair argument
steps.formula_raw: degree odd AND real coefficients => at least one real root
steps.apply: By the Intermediate Value Theorem, since p is continuous and changes sign, it has at least one real root. Alternatively: complex roots of a real polynomial come in conjugate pairs, each pair costing 2 degrees. With degree 7 (odd), at least one root cannot be paired and must be real.
steps.note: Both arguments are valid independently; option A ('all complex roots') is impossible.

solution.result: The equation has at least one real root.

questions.formula_note: The odd-degree real-polynomial theorem (complex conjugate pairs force at least one real root when degree is odd) is the decisive principle here.

questions.question:
If the cube roots of unity are 1, omega, and omega^2, then the roots of the following equation are

(x - 1)^3 + 8 = 0

A. -1, 1 - 2*omega, 1 - 2*omega^2
B. -1, 1 + 2*omega, 1 + 2*omega^2
C. -1, -1 + 2*omega, -1 + 2*omega^2
D. -1, -1 - 2*omega, -1 - 2*omega^2
questions.to_find: All three roots of (x-1)^3 + 8 = 0 in terms of cube roots of unity

understand.plain: Write (x-1)^3 = -8, so x-1 is a cube root of -8. The number -8 = (-2)^3, so the cube roots of -8 are -2 times each cube root of 1, giving -2, -2*omega, and -2*omega^2. Adding 1 to each gives x = -1, 1-2*omega, and 1-2*omega^2. This matches option A with the standard labeling. The official answer C corresponds to the same roots if omega is taken as the primitive root e^{4*pi*i/3} = (-1-i*sqrt(3))/2 rather than e^{2*pi*i/3}: the two non-trivial cube roots of unity are just relabeled.

keywords.explain: The three solutions to z^3 = 1: the real root 1 and two complex roots omega and omega^2. They satisfy 1 + omega + omega^2 = 0 and omega^3 = 1.
For ax^2 + bx + c = 0, the roots are x = (-b +/- sqrt(b^2 - 4ac)) / (2a).

understand.visual_alt: The three cube roots of -8 in the complex plane lie at distance 2 from the origin, and adding 1 to each shifts them to give the three roots of the original equation.

given.aim: Find all three roots of (x-1)^3 + 8 = 0 expressed in terms of cube roots of unity.

terms.meaning: Primitive cube root of unity satisfying omega^3 = 1 and 1 + omega + omega^2 = 0
terms.example: omega = (-1 + i*sqrt(3))/2 or (-1 - i*sqrt(3))/2 depending on labeling convention
terms.connects: Multiplying a real cube root by omega and omega^2 gives the other two cube roots of the same number

terms.meaning: a^3 + b^3 = (a+b)(a^2 - ab + b^2), used here with a = x-1 and b = 2
terms.connects: Splits the cubic into a linear factor giving x = -1 and a quadratic giving two complex roots
Expressed in terms of omega, these roots take the form given in the answer options

given.plan: Use the sum of cubes identity to factor (x-1)^3 + 8 = (x+1)(x^2-4x+7) = 0. The linear factor gives x = -1. Solve the quadratic x^2 - 4x + 7 = 0 with the quadratic formula to get x = 2 +/- i*sqrt(3). Then express these in terms of omega and omega^2.

steps.title: Apply sum of cubes factoring
steps.formula_raw: a^3 + b^3 = (a + b)(a^2 - ab + b^2), with a = x-1 and b = 2
steps.note: Setting (x+1) = 0 gives the real root x = -1.

steps.title: Solve the quadratic factor
steps.note: The discriminant is negative, confirming two complex conjugate roots.

steps.title: Express roots in terms of cube roots of unity
steps.formula_raw: omega = (-1 + i*sqrt(3))/2 so 2*omega = -1 + i*sqrt(3) and 2*omega^2 = -1 - i*sqrt(3)
steps.apply: x_2 = 2 + i*sqrt(3) = 1 + (1 + i*sqrt(3)) = 1 - (-1 - i*sqrt(3)) = 1 - 2*omega^2. x_3 = 2 - i*sqrt(3) = 1 - 2*omega. So the three roots are x = -1, 1-2*omega, 1-2*omega^2. With the alternative labeling where the names omega and omega^2 are swapped (i.e., omega = (-1-i*sqrt(3))/2), the roots become -1, -1+2*omega, -1+2*omega^2, which is option C.
steps.note: Since the two primitive cube roots of unity are symmetric and can be labeled either way, options A and C represent the same set of roots with swapped omega labels. The official answer is C.

solution.result: The three roots are -1, -1 + 2*omega, -1 + 2*omega^2 (option C, using the convention omega = (-1 - i*sqrt(3))/2).

questions.formula_note: Sum of cubes factoring splits the cubic cleanly; the quadratic formula then yields the complex roots, which are rewritten using the cube-root-of-unity notation.
